content.text: Mocking and fixtures are two complementary testing techniques that make agentic AI systems testable without executing real LLM API calls, network requests, or tool invocations. A
is a controlled substitute that replaces a real dependency — an Anthropic client, a database connection, a web-search tool — and returns predetermined, scripted outputs. A
is a piece of pre-baked test data: either a static file (JSON, YAML, or a recorded HTTP cassette) or a pytest setup function that provides a known, reusable state before each test runs.
In agentic AI pipelines the two ideas combine into a layered testing strategy. The
object instead of hitting the network. The

supplies the scripted responses — either authored by hand or recorded from a real session and replayed deterministically. Together they decouple the agent's orchestration logic, tool dispatch, and state management from every unpredictable external dependency, leaving behind a test that runs in milliseconds, costs nothing, and produces the same result on every machine.

The distinction matters: mocking is a runtime substitution technique, while fixtures are the data artifacts that mocks serve. You can have mocks without files (the response is hardcoded in the test) and you can have fixtures without mocks (the fixture loads real data into a real database before a live integration test). In agentic AI testing the two almost always appear together because the recorded API responses that form the fixture are exactly what the mock client replays.
Agentic AI pipelines introduce testing challenges that do not exist in ordinary software. Ordinary unit tests might stub a database or mock an HTTP endpoint; agentic systems must contend with
, high per-call latency, metered API costs, and complex multi-step reasoning loops where each LLM turn can fork the agent's execution path in ways that are difficult to anticipate.

Without mocking, a test suite for an agent that performs three LLM calls per task would require every CI run to make live API requests. If each call averages four seconds of latency and the suite has two hundred test cases, the test run takes over forty minutes before any assertion fires. Costs accumulate: at typical pricing a large suite might spend tens of dollars per day in CI alone. Worse, a rate-limit error or a temporary network blip can fail the entire suite with no signal about whether the code itself is broken.

Non-determinism compounds the problem. The same prompt sent to Claude or GPT-4 on consecutive runs may produce different tool calls, different reasoning chains, or different final answers — not because the code changed, but because of temperature sampling. A test that asserts

can pass on Monday and fail on Tuesday without any code change, producing flaky tests that erode team trust in the test suite. Mocking eliminates this by replacing the LLM with a function that returns the same response every time, making assertions reliable.

Key pain points mocking solves: non-determinism, latency, cost, rate limits, and environment portability. Any one of these is enough to make a test suite impractical without mocking; agentic AI testing faces all five simultaneously.

Think of mocking an LLM like using a flight simulator rather than a real aircraft to train pilots. The simulator does not actually fly — it executes the same decision logic as a real cockpit, but the 'physics engine' (the environment) is a controlled substitute. A trainee's decision-making procedure — the thing being tested — can be exercised safely, cheaply, and repeatably without consuming any real fuel or taking real risk.

The agent under test is the pilot. The mock LLM client is the simulator's instrument panel. The fixtures are the pre-authored scenario scripts: wind shear at minute three, engine warning at minute seven. You can replay any scenario instantly, introduce any edge case deterministically, and test the pilot's logic without ever leaving the ground. The value of the simulator is not that it perfectly replicates flying — it is that it replicates flying
to validate the decision logic while removing everything that makes real flying expensive and dangerous.
A mock always returns the same response to the same input, eliminating test flakiness caused by sampling randomness. Assertions become reliable enough to block merges.
A mock function returns in microseconds; a live LLM call takes three to fifteen seconds. A 200-test suite drops from 40 minutes to under ten seconds.
Tests run freely on every commit, every pull request, and every branch without accumulating token charges.
A failed test always points to agent logic, not a flaky network. The blast radius of an external outage is zero.

You can author fixtures for rare, expensive, or dangerous LLM outputs — empty tool calls, malformed JSON, context-length errors, refusals — that would be impractical or costly to elicit from a real model.
Engineers can write and run tests on laptops with no network access, enabling work on flights, in low-connectivity environments, or behind restrictive corporate proxies.

Golden-file fixtures capture the exact sequence of LLM calls and tool invocations produced by a known-good version of the agent. Any refactor that silently changes the call sequence fails the test immediately.

Over time the real LLM's behavior diverges from recorded fixtures — model updates, new tool schemas, changed system prompts. Fixtures that do not track model versions give false confidence that tests written six months ago still reflect reality.

Mocks test the agent's code paths, not the actual API contract. A breaking API change (a renamed field, a changed error structure) is invisible to mocked tests. Integration tests against the real API remain essential, just not on every commit.

Writing realistic multi-turn fixtures by hand is labour-intensive. A fixture for an agent that uses four tools across three LLM turns might require 150 lines of JSON authored carefully to be internally consistent.
Every change to the agent's system prompt, tool schema, or output format may invalidate existing fixtures. Teams that do not re-record fixtures after changes accumulate silent debt.

Mocked tests cannot discover unexpected emergent behaviours — hallucinations, novel tool-use patterns, creative workarounds — that only appear when the real model runs. Evaluation with live models at the end of a sprint remains indispensable.
Agentic systems with n tool choices per step can branch into
marks: paths. Fixture coverage of all branches is impractical; you must prioritise representative paths and rely on property-based or fuzz testing for the rest.

content.text: directly inside the agent, the agent accepts a client object from the outside. In tests, a mock object is injected; in production, the real client is used. This is the only structural change the agent code requires.
At the mock layer, the substitute client intercepts the
parameter, looks up the appropriate scripted response from its internal queue or a fixture file, and returns a fully-formed
object. The agent receives an object that is structurally identical to what the real API returns — the same fields, the same types, the same
The simplest mock implementation uses a response queue. Before running the agent, the test populates the queue with a list of scripted
objects, one per expected LLM call. The mock's
method dequeues the next response on each invocation. If the agent makes more calls than the queue contains, the mock raises an assertion error, catching unexpected extra calls early.
For a test that expects the agent to make
LLM calls, the fixture must contain exactly
scripted responses. The total test execution time is bounded by the agent's non-LLM compute, not by network latency. If each real LLM call takes
For an agent that makes five LLM calls per run, this translates to a 20,000x speedup per test case.

API interactions during a capture session, serialises them to a fixture file (a cassette), and replays them deterministically in subsequent test runs. This removes the authoring cost of hand-writing fixtures and ensures the recorded responses are structurally valid. The cassette file stores the full request/response pair, so the replay can optionally verify that the agent is sending the same request as during recording — catching prompt regressions.

The diagram below shows the full component graph of a mocked agentic AI test. Solid arrows are real code calls; dashed arrows carry mocked or fixture-loaded data. The agent under test is the only component that is not replaced — its orchestration logic, tool dispatch, and state management all execute for real. Every dependency the agent reaches outward for is intercepted.

attrs.alt: Mocking and Fixtures architecture: test suite invokes agent under test, which calls mock LLM client (fed by fixture store) and mock tool registry; assertions verify call count, arguments, and final output

content.text: — the pytest or unittest harness that sets up fixtures, invokes the agent, and runs assertions.
— the production orchestration code. No changes to business logic. Dependencies are injected, not hardcoded.
— fake implementations of every tool (web search, database query, calculator) that return pre-authored results without side effects.
— JSON or YAML files on disk containing scripted LLM responses and tool results. Loaded by pytest fixtures and injected into mocks before each test.
for agent logic: prompt construction, tool selection heuristics, retry logic, error handling, state transitions.
where speed, cost, and determinism are mandatory.
that are rare, expensive, or impossible to elicit consistently from a real model: malformed JSON outputs, refusals, context overflows, tool call loops.
without network access or API credentials.
after a refactor to verify the agent still issues the same sequence of LLM calls and tool invocations as before.
paths by injecting mock exceptions at specific call positions.
Do not rely on mocking alone when
— whether the agent's answers are accurate, helpful, or well-reasoned. Mocked responses are scripted, not evaluated. Use evaluation frameworks with real models for quality testing.
— new SDK versions, changed field names, or updated error structures are invisible to mocked tests. Run periodic integration tests against the live API.
— novel tool-use strategies, unexpected reasoning patterns, or creative problem-solving that only appear when the real model runs.
in production scenarios. Mocked tests are pathologically fast; they reveal nothing about real-world performance under load.

Refactor the agent to accept the LLM client as a constructor argument rather than instantiating it internally. This is the only change to production code; it makes the agent testable without modifying any logic.
Step 2: Write a mock LLM client
Create a mock class that mirrors the interface of
. It stores a queue of scripted responses and dequeues one per call.
Centralise mock construction in pytest fixtures so tests stay concise. Store fixture data in separate JSON files for reuse across test modules.
With the fixtures in place, tests are concise and readable. Each test focuses entirely on agent logic.

# tests/test_research_agent.py
from agents.research import ResearchAgent
from tests.mocks import MockAnthropicClient, make_text_message


def test_agent_returns_llm_answer(simple_answer_client):
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=simple_answer_client)
    result = agent.run("What is the capital of France?")

    assert result == "The capital of France is Paris."
    simple_answer_client.assert_called_n_times(1)

    # Verify the agent sent the user query in the messages list
    call_args = simple_answer_client.call_log[0]
    assert call_args["messages"][-1]["content"] == "What is the capital of France?"


def test_agent_respects_model_config(simple_answer_client):
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=simple_answer_client, model="claude-haiku-4-5")
    agent.run("test query")

    call_args = simple_answer_client.call_log[0]
    assert call_args["model"] == "claude-haiku-4-5"


def test_agent_retries_on_empty_response():
    """Verify the agent retries when the LLM returns an empty text block."""
    from tests.mocks import make_text_message
    retry_client = MockAnthropicClient(responses=[
        make_text_message(""),           # first call: empty (triggers retry)
        make_text_message("Real answer"),  # second call: valid
    ])
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=retry_client)
    result = agent.run("test query")

    assert result == "Real answer"
    retry_client.assert_called_n_times(2)

# tests/test_tool_agent.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock
from agents.tool_agent import ToolAgent
from tests.mocks import (
    MockAnthropicClient,
    make_text_message,
    make_tool_use_message,
)


@pytest.fixture
def mock_tools():
    """Fake tool implementations with no real side-effects."""
    tools = MagicMock()
    tools.web_search.return_value = {
        "results": [
            {"title": "AI Safety 2025", "snippet": "Key developments this year...", "url": "https://example.com/1"},
            {"title": "Alignment Forum", "snippet": "New paper on RLHF...", "url": "https://example.com/2"},
        ]
    }
    tools.save_note.return_value = {"status": "saved", "note_id": "note_abc"}
    return tools


@pytest.fixture
def research_then_save_client():
    """
    Simulates an agent that:
      Turn 1: calls web_search
      Turn 2: processes results, calls save_note
      Turn 3: confirms completion
    """
    return MockAnthropicClient(responses=[
        make_tool_use_message("web_search", {"query": "AI safety 2025 key papers"}),
        make_tool_use_message("save_note", {"content": "Summary of AI safety research..."}),
        make_text_message("I have searched for recent AI safety papers and saved a summary note."),
    ])


def test_full_research_workflow(research_then_save_client, mock_tools):
    agent = ToolAgent(client=research_then_save_client, tools=mock_tools)
    result = agent.run("Research AI safety and save a summary.")

    # Three LLM calls: search intent, save intent, final answer
    research_then_save_client.assert_called_n_times(3)

    # Each tool was called exactly once
    mock_tools.web_search.assert_called_once()
    mock_tools.save_note.assert_called_once()

    # The web_search was called with the right query
    search_call_args = mock_tools.web_search.call_args[1]
    assert "AI safety" in search_call_args["query"]

    # Final user-facing output
    assert "searched" in result.lower()
    assert "summary" in result.lower()


def test_agent_handles_tool_error_gracefully():
    """If a tool raises, the agent should catch, format the error, and continue."""
    from tests.mocks import make_text_message
    error_client = MockAnthropicClient(responses=[
        make_tool_use_message("web_search", {"query": "test"}),
        make_text_message("The search failed, but I can still help with general knowledge."),
    ])
    failing_tools = MagicMock()
    failing_tools.web_search.side_effect = ConnectionError("network unreachable")

    agent = ToolAgent(client=error_client, tools=failing_tools)
    result = agent.run("Search for something.")

    assert "help" in result.lower()
    error_client.assert_called_n_times(2)
Production scenario: customer support agent test suite

Consider a production customer support agent at a SaaS company. The agent handles 200 distinct ticket categories, uses five tools (order lookup, account status, knowledge base search, ticket creation, escalation), and runs a multi-turn dialogue that averages three LLM calls per conversation. The engineering team has 600 test cases covering common paths, edge cases, and failure modes.
Running these 600 tests live would require 1,800 LLM API calls per CI run. At three seconds per call and a per-call cost of
(input + output tokens averaged), each CI run costs
and takes 90 minutes. With ten engineers pushing commits throughout the day, the daily CI bill exceeds
and the pipeline becomes a bottleneck. The team's solution is a three-tier strategy.

The first tier is the mocked unit test suite. All 600 tests use the MockAnthropicClient and run in under thirty seconds on any developer's laptop. The second tier is a smaller set of 30 integration tests that run nightly against the live API, verifying that the production model still responds compatibly with the agent's parsing code. The third tier is a weekly evaluation run using an LLM-as-judge framework to score output quality on a golden test set.
Mistake 1: Mocking at too high a level
Mocking the entire agent instead of just the LLM client means the orchestration logic never runs in tests.

# WRONG: mocking the agent itself tests nothing
with patch("agents.research.ResearchAgent.run") as mock_run:
    mock_run.return_value = "Paris"
    result = ResearchAgent().run("capital of France?")
    assert result == "Paris"  # trivially true, zero coverage of agent logic

# RIGHT: mock only the external dependency; let agent logic run
mock_client = MockAnthropicClient(responses=[make_text_message("Paris")])
agent = ResearchAgent(client=mock_client)
result = agent.run("capital of France?")
assert result == "Paris"  # agent's parsing, retry, state logic all exercised
Mistake 2: Not asserting on call arguments
Asserting only on the final output lets prompt regressions slip through silently.

# WRONG: only checking output, ignoring what the agent actually sent
def test_agent_output_only(simple_answer_client):
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=simple_answer_client)
    result = agent.run("test")
    assert "Paris" in result  # passes even if the prompt is completely broken

# RIGHT: assert both output and the LLM call arguments
def test_agent_sends_correct_prompt(simple_answer_client):
    agent = ResearchAgent(client=simple_answer_client)
    result = agent.run("capital of France?")

    assert "Paris" in result

    # Verify the system prompt and user message were constructed correctly
    call = simple_answer_client.call_log[0]
    system = call.get("system", "")
    assert "helpful research assistant" in system.lower()
    messages = call["messages"]
    assert messages[0]["role"] == "user"
    assert "capital of France" in messages[0]["content"]
Recording cassettes and never updating them means tests pass confidently while the real API has changed.

# WRONG: cassette recorded 8 months ago, model updated twice since then
# test still passes because it replays old data — but live calls would fail

# RIGHT: stamp cassettes with the model version and re-record when the model changes
CASSETTE_METADATA = {
    "recorded_at": "2025-11-01",
    "model_version": "claude-opus-4-5",   # tie cassette to specific model
    "sdk_version": "0.40.0",
    "responses": [...]
}

# In conftest.py, assert cassette model matches current config:
def load_cassette(name: str, expected_model: str) -> MockAnthropicClient:
    data = json.loads((CASSETTES / f"{name}.json").read_text())
    assert data["model_version"] == expected_model, (
        f"Cassette '{name}' was recorded with {data['model_version']} "
        f"but tests expect {expected_model}. Re-record the cassette."
    )
    return MockAnthropicClient(responses=[_dict_to_message(r) for r in data["responses"]])
. Injection makes the dependency explicit, survives refactors without changing test setup, and works in any test framework.
Assert on call arguments, not only outputs.
Verify that the agent sends the right model, system prompt, max_tokens, and message history. Output-only assertions hide prompt regressions.
Include the model name, SDK version, and recording date in cassette metadata. Fail loudly when the cassette's model does not match the current configuration.
One cassette per logical test scenario, not one cassette for everything. Shared cassettes become fragile and difficult to update.
Write fixtures that trigger context-length errors, rate limits, tool call parsing failures, and malformed JSON returns. These paths are impossible to test reliably with live calls.
Separate mock infrastructure from test logic.
Put MockAnthropicClient, helper constructors, and conftest fixtures in a dedicated
package. Tests should read like specifications, not plumbing.
Run integration tests nightly, not on every commit.
Ten to thirty live-API tests run after hours catch API contract regressions without slowing the main development loop.
Use pytest parametrize for scenario coverage.
A single test function parametrised over ten cassette files gives ten test cases with one assertion pattern, keeping the test suite DRY.

The performance profile of a mocked test suite is dominated by pytest collection overhead and agent Python code, not by I/O. A mock response returns in under 0.1 milliseconds. For a suite of 1,000 tests where each test runs an agent that makes three LLM calls, the total mock execution time is under 0.3 seconds; pytest's own overhead (collection, fixture setup, teardown) typically adds another 2–5 seconds, for a total wall-clock time of under ten seconds.
Scalability concerns arise not in test runtime but in fixture maintenance. As the agent's capability surface grows, the number of distinct scenarios that need cassettes grows with it. If an agent has
turns per conversation, the space of possible interaction paths is bounded by
, which grows exponentially. In practice, teams address this with three strategies.

First, they prioritise high-traffic paths (80% of real conversations follow 20% of paths), writing cassettes for those first. Second, they use property-based testing (Hypothesis) to explore the fixture space programmatically rather than authoring every cassette by hand. Third, they use model-based testing: a state machine describes valid agent transition sequences, and a test generator produces random valid sequences that the mock client serves deterministically.

Fixture file size is rarely a bottleneck. A typical agentic conversation cassette with five LLM turns serialises to 3–8 kilobytes of JSON. A suite of 500 cassettes occupies under 4 megabytes on disk and loads in milliseconds. Parallel test execution with
scales the suite linearly: ten workers run 1,000 tests in the time one worker runs 100.
fixture for convenient patching within tests without manual cleanup.

pip install pytest pytest-mock pytest-xdist

# Run tests in parallel across 4 workers:
pytest tests/ -n 4

# Run only the fast mocked suite, skip integration-tagged tests:
pytest tests/ -m "not integration" -n auto
VCR.py records real HTTP interactions and replays them in tests.

integrates VCR.py with pytest fixtures. While designed for HTTP in general, it works well for Anthropic API calls that go over HTTP — run once to record the cassette, then the suite runs offline forever.

import pytest
import anthropic

@pytest.mark.vcr  # records on first run, replays thereafter
def test_agent_with_vcr():
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # real client; VCR intercepts at the HTTP layer
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=128,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Say hello"}],
    )
    assert "hello" in response.content[0].text.lower()
is a transport-level mock for httpx that intercepts requests before they leave the process. This is more precise than VCR because it does not require a real network call during recording.

import respx
import httpx
import json

@respx.mock
def test_with_respx():
    # Intercept the Anthropic messages endpoint
    respx.post("https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages").mock(
        return_value=httpx.Response(200, json={
            "id": "msg_mock",
            "type": "message",
            "role": "assistant",
            "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Hello from mock!"}],
            "model": "claude-opus-4-5",
            "stop_reason": "end_turn",
            "usage": {"input_tokens": 10, "output_tokens": 5},
        })
    )

    import anthropic
    client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="test-key")
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=128,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}],
    )
    assert response.content[0].text == "Hello from mock!"
Hypothesis generates randomised test inputs that the mock client can serve. This is powerful for testing that the agent's parsing logic handles any valid LLM output structure correctly.

No single approach covers all concerns. The recommended strategy combines all four: mocked unit tests on every commit, cassette-based tests for integration boundaries, live API tests nightly, and LLM-as-judge evaluations weekly or per release.
API key exposure in test fixtures

The most critical risk in fixture-based testing is accidentally including real API keys, authentication tokens, or PII in recorded cassettes. VCR.py cassettes record the full HTTP request including headers — a cassette recorded without sanitisation will contain the
header in plaintext. If cassettes are committed to a public repository, keys are exposed.
Mitigation: always configure VCR.py to filter sensitive headers before recording, and run a pre-commit hook that scans fixture files for patterns resembling API keys.
A mock that does not accurately reflect the real API's error envelope can hide error-handling bugs. If the real API returns
but your mock raises a generic
, the agent's rate-limit retry logic is never exercised. Mock errors using the SDK's actual exception types.

import anthropic

# WRONG: raising a generic exception — does not test the agent's real error path
mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = Exception("rate limit")

# RIGHT: raise the exact exception the SDK raises for rate limits
mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = anthropic.RateLimitError(
    message="Rate limit exceeded",
    response=None,
    body={"error": {"type": "rate_limit_error", "message": "Rate limit exceeded"}},
)

Corrupted or truncated fixture files should fail fast, not silently produce incorrect test results. Add a validation step when loading cassettes that checks structural invariants: every response has a
, content blocks have required fields, and tool_use blocks have IDs that match subsequent tool_result blocks in the conversation.
Mocking replaces external dependencies; fixtures supply the data.
Together they make agentic AI tests deterministic, fast (microseconds per call), and free to run in CI.
Dependency injection is the only required code change.
The agent accepts the LLM client as a constructor argument; tests inject a mock; production injects the real client.
Assert on call arguments, not just final output.
Checking the messages list, model name, and tool schemas that the agent sends catches prompt regressions that output-only assertions miss.
Tie each fixture to a specific model and SDK version. Fail loudly when a test configuration drifts from its cassette's recorded environment.
Mocked tests cannot validate LLM output quality.
They test orchestration logic. Complement with nightly live integration tests and periodic LLM-as-judge evaluation runs.
VCR-recorded cassettes contain full HTTP headers including API keys. Configure header filtering and run pre-commit key-pattern scans.
Use realistic SDK exception types in error tests.
) ensures the agent's error-handling code runs through its real branches.
Scale coverage with pytest parametrize, not more test functions.
One test function parametrised over twenty cassette files is more maintainable than twenty near-identical test functions.
